translation: He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth; like a lamb led to slaughter, and like a sheep before its shearers—silent—so he did not open his mouth.

alternate_translations.translation: He was handled harshly and troubled, but he would not open his mouth—like a lamb brought to the slaughter, like a sheep in front of its shearers, silent—he did not open his mouth.
alternate_translations.notes: “נַעֲנֶה” (na‘anéh) can mean “oppressed/afflicted” or more generally “handled harshly/troubled.” The rest of the wording is a stylistic variation while keeping the meaning.

alternate_translations.translation: He was beaten down and endured suffering, and he did not speak; like a lamb being taken to be slaughtered, like a ewe before her shearers—none will hear a sound—he did not open his mouth.
alternate_translations.notes: “וְלֹא יִפְתַּח־פִּיו” is literally “did not open his mouth,” commonly rendered as not speaking/saying anything. The imagery is preserved, though “ewe” is a gendered alternative to the more neutral “sheep.”

notes: This is a well-known suffering-servant passage (commonly associated with Isaiah 53:7). The repeated phrase “he did not open his mouth” emphasizes complete restraint and silence in the face of injustice. The comparisons to a lamb and a sheep before shearers stress innocence and the absence of resistance.
